analysis.problem: Increasing frequency and severity of extreme humid heat waves, making some areas unlivable
analysis.root_cause: Global warming due to climate change, primarily caused by human activities such as burning fossil fuels and deforestation
leverage_points: Transition to renewable energy sources and increased energy efficiency
Implementation of climate-resilient infrastructure and urban planning

proposed_actions.action: Develop and deploy more energy-efficient air conditioning units and climate-resilient buildings
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Reduce the number of heat-related illnesses and deaths by 20% within the next 5 years

proposed_actions.action: Invest in renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, to reduce greenhouse gas emissions
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Increase the share of renewable energy in the global energy mix to 50% by 2030

evidence: The deadliest heat waves to date have been dry heat waves, but extreme humid heat waves are becoming more frequent and deadly
According to the Children's Climate Risk Report 2026, nearly every child on Earth faces fossil-fueled climate danger
analysis.generalization: This problem connects to the domains of civilization (infrastructure and urban planning), human (health and well-being), and planet (climate and ecosystems)
analysis.summary: The increasing frequency and severity of extreme humid heat waves pose a critical threat to human health and well-being, and require immediate attention and action to transition to renewable energy sources and develop climate-resilient infrastructure

raw_items.title: The Global Risks Report 2026 | World Economic Forum
raw_items.summary: The report analyses global risks through three timeframes to support decision-makers in balancing current crises and longer-term priorities. Chapter 1 presents the findings of this year’s Global Risks Perception Survey (GRPS), which captures insights from over 1,300 experts worldwide.

raw_items.title: Nearly every child on Earth now faces fossil-fueled climate danger
raw_items.summary: The Children’s Climate Risk Report 2026, released Tuesday by the UN Children’s Fund, provides what UNICEF described as “the most detailed global picture to date” of how children are being affected by climate hazards across low-, middle-, and high-income countries.
